seasonal_wine.name: Hermitage rouge en primeur tastings, April
seasonal_wine.description: The major Tain-l'Hermitage negociant houses, led by M. Chapoutier and Maison Paul Jaboulet Aine, open their cellars to the trade each April for en primeur barrel tastings of the previous vintage. Unlike Bordeaux the tastings are informal and release-to-market happens quickly, but April remains the window to assess young Hermitage and Crozes-Hermitage from cask.
seasonal_wine.tip: Contact the domaine direct in February or March. Tastings are predominantly trade and press; private collectors are occasionally accommodated by the larger houses.

verified.address_quoted: 18 avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

seasonal_wine.name: Condrieu new vintage release, March to May
seasonal_wine.description: Condrieu Viognier is bottled young and released for drinking within one to three years of harvest. The new vintage releases each spring, typically March through May, with producers including Georges Vernay, Yves Cuilleron, and Domaine Pierre Gaillard opening for direct sales. Condrieu is best approached young when its hallmark apricot-blossom aromatics are freshest; the release window is the ideal buying moment.
seasonal_wine.tip: Book a tasting at Georges Vernay's Condrieu cellar for direct allocation of limited-production single-parcel wines that rarely reach export markets.

seasonal_wine.name: Tavel rose new vintage release, March to April
seasonal_wine.description: Tavel, France's only AOC exclusively dedicated to dry rose, releases its new vintage between March and April. The wine is made primarily from Grenache, Cinsault, and Clairette and is one of the most age-worthy roses in France, though most producers advise drinking within three years. Spring releases are celebrated at the cave cooperative and independent domaines around the village of Tavel, 15 km north-west of Avignon.
seasonal_wine.tip: Unlike most Provence roses, Tavel's fuller body pairs well with lamb and charcuterie. Stock up on new-release bottles for summer entertaining.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison of the vines and the Fete de la Veraison, August
seasonal_wine.description: Veraison, the colour change of ripening grapes, occurs in the Rhone around late July to early August. Chateauneuf-du-Pape celebrates this moment with the Fete de la Veraison, a medieval-themed harvest festival in the village. The vines in their pre-harvest state make August an ideal time to walk the galets roulants (large smooth pebbles) soils of Chateauneuf-du-Pape and Gigondas and see the nearly-ripe bunches in the vineyard.
what_to_taste: Gigondas en vrac at domaine cellars
seasonal_wine.tip: The Fete de la Veraison offers medieval banquets, grape treading, and processions through the village. Book accommodation in Avignon weeks in advance as hotel supply is thin in August.

seasonal_wine.name: Syrah harvest in the Northern Rhone, September
seasonal_wine.description: Syrah harvest on the steep northern Rhone slopes is entirely manual. The terraced granite terroirs of Cote-Rotie, Hermitage, and Cornas require hand-picking with baskets, with equipment carried up narrow pathways. Visitors to Tain-l'Hermitage and Ampuis in mid-September will see pickers filing up the hill at dawn. Several estates offer paid harvest experiences where participants join the picking crew for a morning, followed by a cellar lunch.
what_to_taste: Cote-Rotie new-harvest barrel sample at Guigal
Hermitage rouge new harvest at Chapoutier
seasonal_wine.tip: Book harvest experiences with M. Chapoutier or E. Guigal at least two months in advance. The physical work is strenuous on the steep slopes.

verified.address_quoted: 18 avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

seasonal_wine.name: Grenache harvest in Chateauneuf-du-Pape and Gigondas, September to October
seasonal_wine.description: Grenache dominates in Chateauneuf-du-Pape, Gigondas, and Vacqueyras, and its harvest typically follows Syrah by one to three weeks. The distinctive galets roulants (rolled pebbles) soils of Chateauneuf accumulate heat through the day and release it at night, promoting even ripening. Late-harvest Grenache from old vines reaches natural alcohol levels of 14.5 to 16 percent. Estate visits are discouraged at the height of harvest but the Chateauneuf-du-Pape syndicat organises post-harvest open-cellar events.
seasonal_wine.tip: Chateau Rayas maintains some of the oldest surviving ungrafted Grenache vines in France and is open by appointment only. Request a visit well in advance.

seasonal_wine.name: Ban des Vendanges: the official harvest opening
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, late September or early October
seasonal_wine.description: The Ban des Vendanges is the official announcement by the appellation authority of the opening of harvest. In the Rhone, the Ban is declared separately for each major AOC based on maturity assessments by the syndicat and the INAO. In Chateauneuf-du-Pape the Ban is traditionally marked by a small ceremony. The date varies by vintage character, typically falling between late September and early October, and sets the legal start date for commercial harvest. Some communes include a public event.
seasonal_wine.tip: The Ban is a local institutional event more than a public spectacle, but checking the date helps visitors plan around the peak harvest window when cellar gates are often closed.

seasonal_wine.name: Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise VDN autumn release
seasonal_wine.description: Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise, one of France's oldest Vin Doux Naturel (VDN) appellations, releases its new vintage in late October and November. The fortified Muscat Blanc a Petits Grains is harvested ripe, fermented partially, and fortified with neutral grape spirit to preserve sweetness and Muscat aromatics. The resulting wine is golden, intensely perfumed with orange blossom and stone fruit, and best drunk young. The Beaumes-de-Venise village cave cooperative and several small domaines offer direct cellar sales.
seasonal_wine.tip: Pair Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise with foie gras, Roquefort, or fresh fruit tarts at the Beaumes-de-Venise autumn harvest table. The co-operative offers tastings year-round.

seasonal_wine.name: Rasteau VDN fortified winter release, November to December
seasonal_wine.description: Rasteau AOC produces both a dry red appellation and Rasteau VDN, a Grenache-based fortified wine available in blanc, rouge, and rancio styles. The rancio-style Rasteau, which undergoes deliberate oxidative ageing in glass demijohns outside, is among France's most characterful fortified wines and a direct parallel to Madeira or Tawny Port. The VDN release in November and December makes it ideal for winter aperitif and dessert pairings. Domaine de la Soumade and the Rasteau cave cooperative are the principal producers.
what_to_taste: Rasteau Domaine de la Soumade Orondasso
seasonal_wine.tip: The rancio style is an acquired taste but revelatory with walnut cake and aged Comté. Ask the cave cooperative for a vertical tasting of the oxidative style across several vintages.

seasonal_wine.name: Winter cellar season: racking and assemblage in Tain and Avignon
seasonal_wine.description: January and February are the working months in Rhone cellars: racking of the previous harvest's wines, assemblage (blending) decisions for Chateauneuf-du-Pape's multi-variety blends, and the final preparations for spring bottling. Visitors who come in winter find the northern Rhone stripped bare, the Hermitage hill gaunt above the river, and the cellar doors open for focused, unhurried tastings. Several producers including Domaine Jean-Louis Chave and Domaine Auguste Clape are most accessible to appointment visits during this quiet period.
seasonal_wine.tip: Winter visits require advance appointments but often provide access to working barrels and the winemaker's attention that harvest visits cannot.

verified.address_quoted: 18 avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

seasonal_wine.name: Decouverte en Vallee du Rhone, April, Avignon
seasonal_wine.description: Inter Rhone's professional tasting event brings together producers from both banks of the Rhone each April in Avignon. Decouverte en Vallee du Rhone is primarily a trade and press tasting where buyers, sommeliers, and journalists assess the latest releases from across all Rhone appellations. Around 200 producers pour across two days. The event is the largest professional forum for Rhone Valley wines and an important moment for retailers and importers to build their annual Rhone lists.
seasonal_wine.tip: Public access is limited; apply via Inter Rhone with professional credentials. The event is held at the Palais des Papes or Avignon convention centre depending on the year.

seasonal_wine.name: Chateauneuf-du-Pape blanc early release, March to April
seasonal_wine.description: Chateauneuf-du-Pape blanc, made primarily from Grenache Blanc, Clairette, Roussanne, and Bourboulenc, is typically bottled and released earlier than the rouge. Many producers release their blanc in February or March after short barrel or stainless-steel ageing. The blanc of Chateau Beaucastel (notably Roussanne Vieilles Vignes), Chateau Rayas Blanc, and Domaine du Vieux Telegraphe Blanc are among the most sought. Spring visits to the region coincide with the blanc release window.
seasonal_wine.tip: Chateau Beaucastel's Roussanne Vieilles Vignes blanc is released in very limited quantity; cellar-door and direct mailing-list allocation is the surest route.
